title: How to Analyze Seasonal Income Using Bank Statements
meta: Seasonal businesses and workers present unique challenges for bank statement analysis. Learn how lenders calculate income, adjust for seasonality, and assess risk for seasonal borrowers.
content:
The Seasonal Income Challenge

Millions of self-employed workers, small business owners, and contractors earn income that is heavily concentrated in certain months and minimal or absent in others. A landscaper earns 80% of their revenue from April through October. A holiday retailer does most of their business in Q4. A tax preparer is busy January through April.

Standard bank statement analysis — averaging 3 months of deposits — fails for these borrowers unless the 3 months are chosen carefully to represent their full annual pattern.

Why Standard 3-Month Analysis Fails for Seasonal Borrowers

Imagine analyzing a landscaper's bank statements from November through January. You'd see near-zero revenue and might conclude the business isn't viable. But the same business analyzed in June through August might show $20,000+ in monthly revenue.

Neither window tells the full story. The only way to accurately assess a seasonal borrower is to review a full annual cycle — at minimum 12 months of statements.

The 12-Month Method for Seasonal Income

The standard approach for seasonal borrowers:

  1. Collect 12 months of bank statements
  2. Sum all qualifying deposits for the full 12-month period
  3. Divide by 12 to get average monthly income
  4. Verify the annual total against business tax returns (Schedule C, 1120-S, 1065)

This captures both peak and off-season months, producing a true average that reflects the business's actual annual capacity.

Seasonal Adjustments in Bank Statement Mortgages

Bank statement mortgage programs for self-employed borrowers specifically accommodate seasonal income by requiring 24 months of statements and using the 24-month average. This further smooths out year-to-year variation caused by particularly strong or weak years.

Verifying Seasonal Income Management

Beyond calculating average income, lenders should assess how the borrower manages their cash flow during off-season months:

  • Savings buffer: Does the account maintain a sufficient balance during off-season months to cover obligations?
  • Line of credit usage: Some seasonal businesses use revolving credit during slow periods — high utilization during off-season is expected but should improve during peak season.
  • Expense management: Are expenses reduced during slow months (variable cost structure) or do fixed costs continue regardless of revenue?

Industries with Common Seasonal Patterns

  • Construction and landscaping: High April–October, low November–March
  • Retail and e-commerce: High October–December, lower January–September
  • Tax preparation: High January–April, low other months
  • Tourism and hospitality: Varies by geography and type
  • Agriculture: Highly variable by crop and region
  • Accounting: High January–April, October–December

Common Red Flags in Seasonal Business Analysis

While seasonal income itself is not a risk, these patterns within seasonal businesses warrant scrutiny:

  • Peak season revenue declining year-over-year
  • Off-season balances reaching zero or going negative
  • MCA debt taken on during slow periods indicating a cash flow crisis
  • No reserve buildup during peak months despite stated high revenue

AI Tools for Seasonal Analysis

Analyzing 12–24 months of bank statements manually is time-prohibitive. StatementScrub processes multiple months simultaneously, calculating true annualized averages, identifying seasonal patterns, and flagging off-season financial stress — making seasonal borrower underwriting practical at scale.
